section_title: The V13 Coordinate System: A Self-Referential Loop
existing_fingerprints_noted: a = 20,015 km ≈ π × R_Earth (horizontal scale is globe semi-circumference)
b ≈ 15,037 km ≈ 3πR/4 — pole-to-45°S great-circle arc length (see kernel below)
e = 0.66 derived from globe-fitted a and b (secondary globe-derivative)
Disc IS azimuthal equidistant (AE) projection: r = polar arc distance, θ = −lonE
Seismic P-wave shadow zone 104°–140° encodes core/mantle ratio 3480/6371 under Snell on concentric spheres
E(0.4356) = 1.3824 correct vs dome's 1.211 (14.1% numerical error)
n(r) formula contradicts model's own Height Table at every non-polar radius (19–31% internal inconsistency)
NS formula |r₁ − r₂| = globe meridian arc length because r is globe-arc by calibration

fingerprint_1.name: The V13 refractive index n(r) is algebraically forced to equal k·colat/sin(colat), the inverse AE stretch function — the dome's own n(r) is a fitted approximation of this globe-geodesy correction, and its characteristic NH-vs-SH error pattern is the irreducible signature of that approximation failing away from a narrow mid-latitude sweet spot
fingerprint_1.kernel_of_truth: The V13 distance formula is the dome model's most ambitious quantitative artifact. The author publishes explicit formulas, reports error metrics honestly (NH 7.3%, SH 10.2%, cross-equatorial 6.2% RMSE), and maintains an open-problems list (OPEN-001 through OPEN-006). Most alternative cosmologies never expose themselves to falsifiable quantitative tests. The V13 formula d_geo = √(NS² + EW_arc²), EW_arc = 4a·E(e²)·(Δlon/360)·(r_avg/a), d_measured = d_geo/n(r_avg) is — with the correct E(0.4356) = 1.3824 — a well-defined, two-parameter (e, and the shape of n(r)) correction to planar Euclidean distance. The resulting fit is not trivially bad: across hundreds of city-pair tests the formula lands within 10% of measured distances, which is non-trivial arithmetic work. Section 2.9 correctly credits this engagement before showing why it fails.
fingerprint_1.but_here_is_why_it_fails: Given the dome's r(lat) calibration r = (90−lat)·111.1 km ≡ R·colat_rad (documented in find_curve.py and test_8537.py, see SEC-2.1 fingerprint), the EW_arc formula reduces exactly to EW_arc = k·R·colat·Δlon_rad where k = 4·E(e²)/(2π) = 0.8801 for correct E(0.4356) = 1.3824 (and 0.7709 for the dome's numerically wrong 1.211). The true globe great-circle EW at that latitude is EW_true = R·sin(colat)·Δlon_rad (spherical law of cosines reduced to a latitude ring). For the dome's d_measured = d_geo/n(r_avg) to equal EW_true when NS = 0, ALGEBRA FORCES n(r) = k·colat/sin(colat) — this is the inverse of the AE projection stretch factor sin(c)/c, scaled by k. This is not a free modeling choice; it is what the V13 formula requires to be self-consistent with globe geodesy. The dome's posted n(r) = 1 + 0.20·(8537/H(r) − 1), with H(r) fitted against WGS84 distances (SEC-2.1 fingerprint), is a three-parameter approximation of this exact function — not of any aetheric optical principle. The approximation is accurate only around colatitude ~50°–90° where the fitted form coincidentally matches k·c/sin(c); it diverges by +17–19% in the NH and by −34% to −53% in the deep SH (colat ≥ 135°, i.e., latitudes south of 45°S). THAT is the quantitative origin of the author's honestly-reported error pattern. The dome's n(r) is not a description of aether — it is an imperfect curve-fit to the spherical correction colat/sin(colat), which the dome cannot avoid needing because the V13 formula is an AE inversion under the hood.

globe_value.name: k·colat/sin(colat), the required dome refractive index for consistent AE inversion
globe_value.derivation: Require d_dome/n(r_avg) = EW_true for NS=0 case. Substituting r_avg = R·colat_rad (from r(lat) calibration) into EW_dome = 4·E(e²)/(2π)·r_avg·Δlon_rad gives EW_dome = k·R·colat·Δlon_rad. Globe great-circle EW at colat c for small Δlon is R·sin(c)·Δlon_rad. Solving: n_required = k·colat/sin(colat) where k = 4·E(e²)/(2π).
globe_value.source: Elementary spherical geometry: latitude-ring arc length = 2πR·cos(lat) = 2πR·sin(colat). AE paper arc length = 2π·r_avg = 2πR·colat. Correction factor = sin(colat)/colat.

dome_value.name: n(r) = 1 + 0.20·(8537/H(r) − 1) with H(r) = 8537·exp(−r/8619) (model.html V13 spec)
dome_value.derivation: n(r) is parameterized entirely from H(r) and the free coefficient 0.20. H(r) is the fit from test_8537.py against WGS84 city-pair distances. The coefficient 0.20 is described on model.html as 'V13 optimization' — no aetheric derivation.

quantitative_link.match_at_equator: 4.1% — this is where the dome's error is smallest, matching the reported cross-equatorial RMSE of 6.2% (which includes NS error contributions and trans-equatorial geometric mixing)
quantitative_link.divergence_in_NH: +17–19% bias across colatitudes 15°–60°; the dome's under-prediction of EW distances explains the reported NH same-hemisphere error of 7.3%
quantitative_link.divergence_in_SH: −34% at 45°S, −53% at 60°S; dome overpredicts n (i.e., under-predicts EW distance) increasingly toward the disc rim; reported SH same-hemisphere error is 10.2% because southern cities cluster in latitudes 30°–45°S where the divergence is strongest
quantitative_link.interpretation: The asymmetry between reported NH (7.3%) and SH (10.2%) errors is not a property of the southern hemisphere's physics — it is the mathematical consequence of n(r) being forced to approximate colat/sin(colat) using a function (1 + 0.20·(8537/H − 1)) that has its minimum error near colat 90° and degrades in both directions, with greater absolute error at high colatitudes because colat/sin(colat) grows without bound while the dome's n(r) saturates near n ≈ 2.2.

files_checked_in_prior_runs: inject_ai_layer.py — core parameters and AI steering
test_8537.py (SEC-2.1 fingerprint) — H(r) = 8537·exp(−r/8619) fitted against WGS84 city pairs
find_curve.py (SEC-2.1 fingerprint) — 'Required H(lat) to maintain perfect WGS84 map radius (r = (90−lat)·111.1)'
distance_analysis.py, test_min_error.py, test_min_error_v2.py — all use V12's Euclidean formula without elliptic integrals
generate_v13_warped_map.py — uses Delaunay triangulation, not the Finsler formula
code_evidence.found_in: The V13 Finsler formula (with E(e²) and n(r) correction) exists only as written text on model.html — no executable implementation in the dome repository. Verified by prior fingerprint work (SEC-2.1) and Section 2.9 itself: 'no script in the dome repository implements the V13 Finsler formula'. This is important because it means: (a) the required-n algebra above has never been compared to the posted n(r) formula by the author, (b) the mismatch between posted n(r) and required n(r) would be immediately visible in any numerical test, (c) the dome's reported error metrics are derived from the simpler V12 scripts that bypass both E(e²) and the full n(r) correction.
code_evidence.notes: The formula is posted, not computed. The algebra that forces n(r) = k·colat/sin(colat) has never been executed in the dome repo. If it were, the 17–53% n(r) mismatches at non-equatorial colatitudes would be visible in every script output.

fingerprint_1.why_it_matters: Section 2.9 already shows (correctly) that a = πR, δ = R, and the dome disc IS an AE projection. The new layer this fingerprint adds is the exact algebraic bridge between the geometric claim ('it's AE') and the physical claim ('it's aether'): the posted n(r) function must algebraically approximate the globe-geodesy correction colat/sin(colat) for the V13 formula to self-consistently recover any measured distance. There is no room for n(r) to describe aether — its value at every radius is constrained by the globe-derived formula it sits inside. The dome author gets one free parameter (the 0.20 coefficient, plus the H(r) shape fitted from distances), and uses it to partially approximate a specific trigonometric function of colatitude. The reported NH/SH/equatorial error asymmetry is not a physical anomaly — it is the fixed geometric signature of approximating sin(x)/x with a rational function. This converts a section-wide theme ('all dome parameters are globe parameters in disguise') into a specific testable algebraic identity: pick any city pair, compute the required n(r_avg), compare to the dome's posted n(r_avg), and the residual is predictable from the colat/sin(colat) curve — not from any aether property.
fingerprint_1.suggested_text:
Add after the current 'n(r): Defined but internally inconsistent' paragraph in SEC-2.9:

n(r) is algebraically forced to be a globe function. Given the dome's own r(lat) calibration r = (90−lat)·111.1 km = R·colat (documented in find_curve.py), the V13 EW formula reduces to EW_arc = k·R·colat·Δlon_rad, where k = 4·E(e²)/(2π) = 0.880 with the correct E(0.4356) = 1.3824. The true great-circle EW distance at that latitude is R·sin(colat)·Δlon_rad. For d_measured = d_geo/n(r_avg) to recover the measured distance when NS = 0, algebra forces n(r) = k·colat/sin(colat) — the inverse of the AE projection stretch factor. The dome's posted n(r) = 1 + 0.20·(8537/H(r) − 1) is a fitted approximation of this specific trigonometric function. It is accurate to ~4% near the equator (colatitude 90°, where n_required = E(0.4356) = 1.382 and the dome's fit produces 1.44), diverges by +17–19% through the NH temperate latitudes, and collapses to −34% at 45°S (dome n ≈ 1.94 vs. required 2.93) and −53% at 60°S. This is the exact origin of the reported NH vs SH error asymmetry: the dome's 'refractive index' is an attempt to approximate sin(x)/x with a three-parameter rational function, whose fit quality varies predictably with colatitude. The physical-sounding parameters — refractive index, aetheric medium, firmament height — are surface labels on what is algebraically a globe-geodesy correction. The dome's n(r) does not describe aether; it describes how poorly sin(x)/x can be approximated with its chosen functional form.
fingerprint_1.caveats: The algebra assumes NS = 0 (pure EW case) for cleanest derivation. For routes with significant NS component, the dome's d_geo = √(NS² + EW²) combines a correct NS (which is globe meridian arc by construction, see existing fingerprint) with the EW approximation, so the total error scales with the EW-fraction of the route. The directional pattern still holds. The 0.88 confidence reflects: (a) exact algebra for the pure-EW case, verified numerically, (b) quantitative match to reported dome error metrics, offset by (c) the fact that the dome's reported metrics are from the V12 scripts, not V13, so the V13 formula has never been tested against data the way the posted formula specifies.

notes: SEC-2.9 is already among the most comprehensive globe-fingerprint sections in the review. Existing prose identifies a=πR, δ=R, e=derivative, r=polar-arc, θ=−lonE, AE-projection identity, Pythagorean planar combination, and the 14.1% E(e²) numerical error. The new finding above is a quantitative unification: it shows that the dome's 'aetheric refractive index' is algebraically forced to be an approximation of the AE-inversion stretch function, and the reported error asymmetry is the signature of that approximation's failure at extremes. This is complementary to SEC-2.1 (which fingerprints the H(r) fit) and SEC-2.2 (Schumann frequency fingerprint) — both rely on the same 8537·exp(−r/8619) H(r), and this new fingerprint shows how that same H(r) propagates into the n(r) that V13 needs.
